Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd tell myself I was going to study at certain times, but then I'd just... drift. I spent about 23 hours a week supposedly studying, but a lot of that was me sitting with materials open while my mind was somewhere else. I'd read the same paragraph multiple times without absorbing anything. It felt like going through the motions more than actually learning.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Sometimes I'd go days without touching anything, then suddenly realize a deadline was coming and try to cram everything in. I skipped a lot of classes—like, almost half of them—so I was constantly playing catch-up. The inconsistency made everything feel chaotic, even when I technically had time.
Q3: Usually panic, if I'm being real. I'd get an email reminder about something due or realize I hadn't checked in for a while and feel this wave of dread. Sometimes I logged in just to ease my anxiety, to see what I was missing. It wasn't about being proactive—it was more like damage control.
Q4: For a few weeks in the middle of the semester, I actually got into a rhythm of checking the LMS in the mornings. I watched a ton of videos during that stretch—hours of them. But it didn't last. Once I fell behind again and started feeling overwhelmed, logging in just reminded me how much I was failing, so I avoided it.
Q5: The lecture videos were the most useful for me because I could pause and rewind when I zoned out. Since I missed so many classes, they were basically my lifeline. I'm more of a read-write learner, but the videos helped me understand context before I tried to read through the materials. I watched over 160 minutes of them, which is probably more than I spent in actual class.
Q6: I basically ignored the discussion boards. I posted once the entire semester, and even that felt forced. I just couldn't bring myself to engage with other students—it felt performative and stressful. The peer interaction stuff felt like extra work that wouldn't help my grade directly, so I kept putting it off until I just stopped thinking about it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or exam. If I could see the immediate payoff, I'd do it. But anything that seemed like it was just for 'enrichment' or building understanding long-term? I couldn't motivate myself. I know that's short-sighted, but when you're scared and overwhelmed, you just focus on survival.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap, honestly. Like, they're supposedly optional, but then you wonder if you're missing something crucial. I mostly skipped them because I was already struggling with the required stuff. Required activities had deadlines attached, which gave me structure even if I hated it. Optional stuff just floated in this anxiety-inducing limbo.
Q9: Yeah, I did a lot of studying that wouldn't show up anywhere. I'd download PDFs and readings, then work through them on my tablet without being connected. I also used my own notes and sometimes looked things up on YouTube when the course materials didn't click. A lot of my actual learning happened offline, away from the LMS.
Q10: There were whole weekends where I'd print out readings and sit in my room going through them with a highlighter. The LMS would show nothing for those days. Or I'd rewatch downloaded lecture videos on my phone during commutes. I was engaging with the content, just not in any way the system could track.
Q11: Definitely. I'd open a reading once on the LMS, download it, and then never go back to that page even though I read the document three or four times. Same with some video transcripts—I'd copy them into a Word doc and study from there. The LMS only saw me access it once, but I used it way more than that.
Q12: Around midterms, everything fell apart for me. I was scared—like genuinely scared—about failing, and that fear paralyzed me instead of motivating me. I started avoiding the course because looking at it made me feel sick. The CBT sessions helped a bit with managing those thoughts, but by then I'd already dug myself into a hole.
Q13: Absolutely. I was taking five online courses simultaneously, which was way too much. The workload was crushing, and I constantly felt confused about what was due when. Fatigue made it hard to focus even when I sat down to study. I'd log into the LMS, see the mountain of tasks, and just shut my laptop.
Q14: All the time. I'd scroll through materials, click on things, maybe start a video, but my brain was completely checked out. I was physically present but mentally absent. It's like my body was going through the motions while my mind was somewhere else, worrying about everything I was failing at.
Q15: I postponed the bigger assignments constantly, even though I knew they were important. The fear of doing them badly made me avoid starting at all. It's this weird thing where the more something matters, the scarier it is to attempt. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd say the same thing.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me do anything. I'd ignore tasks until the last possible moment, then scramble to submit something. My LMS activity probably spiked right before due dates and flatlined in between. It wasn't healthy, but without that external pressure, I couldn't make myself engage.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss. Sometimes getting a grade back motivated me to try harder on the next thing. But when the feedback was bad—which it often was—it just confirmed my fears that I couldn't do this. After a while, I started dreading feedback more than wanting it.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with five online courses going at once. There was so much content and so many different platforms and deadlines to track. I needed more scaffolding, like smaller chunks with clearer guidance. The way it was set up assumed I had my life together, which I definitely didn't.
Q19: The flexibility was supposed to be a plus, but for someone like me, it was actually harder. I needed more structure, not less. The discussion requirements felt arbitrary, and the way materials were organized made it hard to know what to prioritize. Clearer pathways would have helped me feel less lost.
Q20: That sounds like me, honestly. You can be clicking around, watching videos, reading things, but if you're not actually processing it or translating it into completed work, the activity means nothing. High activity might just mean someone's anxious and checking things repeatedly, not that they're learning or producing.
Q21: I can see that happening if someone's efficient or studies offline a lot. They might download everything, work through it on their own, and only log in to submit. Their LMS footprint would be tiny, but they could still be doing the work. The system doesn't capture that kind of engagement at all.
Q22: Logs can show patterns—like when you log in, what you click, how long you spend. But they can't show whether you're actually thinking or just staring at the screen. They miss all the offline work, the emotional state, the quality of engagement. It's quantity data, not quality data.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it. It's not just doing tasks—it's wanting to understand, being curious, feeling like the work matters. For me, I rarely felt that this semester. I was going through motions out of fear, not genuine engagement.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I was all semester, or how that fear made me avoid things instead of face them. It wouldn't show the offline studying I did, or how hard I tried during the CBT sessions to get my head right. It also wouldn't show that even though I failed, I didn't stop caring—I just couldn't figure out how to make it work.
